summary:
Charlemagne's Empire (c. 768–814 AD) was the dominant political force in early medieval Europe — a Frankish state that under King (later Emperor) Charlemagne expanded to encompass modern France, Belgium, the Netherlands, Germany, Austria, Switzerland, northern Italy, and northeastern Spain, creating the largest western European polity since the Roman Empire. Charlemagne (Charles the Great, r. 768–814 AD) inherited a substantial kingdom from his father Pepin III and expanded it through decades of nearly continuous campaigning: the 30-year Saxon Wars (converting the Saxons to Christianity by force), campaigns against the Lombards, Avars, and Slavs, and defensive campaigns in Spain.

Charlemagne's importance was not merely military. His court at Aachen was the center of the [[event:2140|Carolingian Renaissance]], a deliberate revival of classical learning, standardized script, educational reform, and church organization. His administrative system — using counts as regional representatives and missi dominici as inspectors — attempted to govern a vast pre-literate empire through loyalty networks. His [[event:2139|coronation as Emperor]] on Christmas Day 800 AD by Pope Leo III created the concept of the Holy Roman Empire, the medieval 'successor' to Rome in the west, and established the uneasy relationship between papacy and emperor that would dominate European politics for centuries. The [[event:2141|Treaty of Verdun]] (843 AD) divided his empire among his grandsons, creating the political outlines of modern France and Germany.
